name: Pat LaBarbera
abstract: Pat (Pascel Emmanuel) LaBarbera (born April 7, 1944) is an American-born Canadian jazz tenor, alto and soprano saxophonist, clarinetist, and flautist born in Mt. Morris, New York, most notable for his work as a soloist in Buddy Rich bands from 1967 to 1973.

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Pat LaBarbera in the context of a reissued album; the interviewee (Elvin Jones) does not mention LaBarbera by name nor confirm personal interaction, only discussing the reissue process.
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Gap Mangione
about_quotes.evidence: And when I ended up with just a saxophone section, Pat LaBarbera and Andy Weinzler from Buffalo... Of course Pat LaBarbera would come down very often to join us.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gap Mangione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Rochester, New York, January 11, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Pat LaBarbera called me. He said, 'I’ve got to tell you. Buddy had a big meeting after you left... Pat LaBarbera says, 'Louie says you did.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: I can remember Pat LaBarbera saying to me at Louisville – he said, “How do you like that student I sent you, Ralph Bowen?”
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Don Menza and Sam Noto
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] He had upstate people too. Joe Romano, Pat LaBarbera, Sal— [INTERVIEWEE] Sal Nistico played for him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Don Menza and Sam Noto interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 28, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Tim Ries
about_quotes.evidence: It was after Pat LaBarbera had stopped playing with him, who was a great musician from Upstate New York.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tim Ries interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, March 19, 2022
